For bandit market learning, we will integrate TR with two-point feedback schemes to build models of the equilibrium payoff function, ensuring progress despite noise. Complexity analysis will focus on iteration and sample complexity to reach an approximate stationary point.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield new, provably convergent algorithms for challenging optimization paradigms where derivatives are inaccessible. It will provide a unified analysis connecting derivative-free optimization with modern learning and equilibrium problems, offering practical, theoretically grounded tools for researchers in optimization and machine learning.
proposals.novelty: While trust-region methods are classic in derivative-free optimization, their application and analysis for bilevel optimization and for learning equilibria under bandit feedback is novel. The key innovation is the careful management of the trust region alongside the estimation errors inherent in these hierarchical or feedback-limited problems.

proposals.title: Communication-Efficient Distributed Zeroth-Order Optimization via Smoothed Gradient-Free Methods
proposals.motivation: Large-scale optimization in settings like hyperparameter tuning, reinforcement learning, and complex simulation-based engineering often relies on gradient-free (zeroth-order) methods, as gradients are unavailable or prohibitively expensive. Scaling these methods to modern distributed computing environments is critical, but existing distributed zeroth-order algorithms suffer from high communication overhead and lack rigorous convergence guarantees for nonsmooth, nonconvex objectives prevalent in real-world applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a distributed, parallel version of the Smoothed Gradient-Free Method (SGFM), combined with variance reduction and adaptive synchronization protocols, can achieve near-linear speedup in convergence while maintaining provable guarantees for nonsmooth, nonconvex problems, even with bandit-like feedback and sparse communication.
proposals.method: We will design a master-worker architecture where each worker performs local SGFM steps using two-point bandit feedback. The core innovation is a novel communication protocol that asynchronously aggregates smoothed function value differences instead of full gradient estimates, drastically reducing bandwidth. We will incorporate a variance-reduced estimator inspired by stochastic compositional optimization techniques to control the error from zeroth-order approximations. The convergence analysis will extend recent deterministic nonsmooth nonconvex optimization frameworks to this distributed, stochastic setting, explicitly characterizing the trade-off between parallelism, communication cost, and optimization error.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first provably convergent, communication-efficient distributed algorithm for zeroth-order optimization in nonsmooth nonconvex settings. It will deliver a practical open-source implementation and a comprehensive theoretical analysis linking synchronization frequency, sample complexity, and network topology, directly addressing scalability bottlenecks in large-scale simulation and learning.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in merging the SGFM framework—designed for nonsmooth problems—with distributed computing primitives for bandit feedback, moving beyond the smooth convex distributed optimization literature. The communication protocol based on transmitting function value differences, rather than parameter vectors or gradient estimates, is a non-obvious and efficient design for the zeroth-order setting.

proposals.title: Online Learning for Nonsmooth Nonconvex Objectives: Regret Analysis and Algorithm Design
proposals.motivation: Many modern learning and decision-making problems, from strategic interactions in dynamic markets to training complex neural architectures, involve optimizing objectives that are both nonconvex and nonsmooth. While recent work has advanced deterministic methods for such static problems, the online setting—where the function sequence is revealed sequentially—remains largely unexplored theoretically. This gap limits the application of these tools to real-time systems where data or objectives evolve, such as online resource allocation or adaptive control.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by leveraging a careful combination of inexact hypergradient ideas and online-to-batch conversion techniques, it is possible to design an online algorithm for nonsmooth nonconvex functions that achieves sublinear regret, even when only bandit or partial feedback is available.
proposals.method: We will adapt the deterministic analysis of nonsmooth nonconvex optimization to an online framework. The core technical approach involves defining a suitable regret metric based on the Moreau envelope or Goldstein stationarity. We will then develop an online algorithm using a proximal or clipped gradient scheme, incorporating ideas from inexact hypergradient descent for bilevel problems to handle the nonsmoothness. The analysis will draw from tools in online convex optimization and complexity theory, extending them to the nonconvex setting with a focus on bounding the expected regret over time.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous regret guarantees for online nonsmooth nonconvex optimization, extending the theoretical foundations of online learning. It will yield a practical algorithmic framework applicable to dynamic market equilibria and adaptive learning problems where objectives lack convexity and smoothness. The results will bridge the gap between deterministic nonconvex optimization and online learning theory.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in merging the challenging, under-explored domain of online nonconvex optimization with the additional complexity of nonsmoothness, moving beyond the typical online convex or smooth nonconvex settings. The proposed use of stationarity-based regret and techniques from bilevel optimization to handle inexactness in an online bandit feedback loop is a non-obvious synthesis of Dr. Jordan's recent methodological themes.

proposals.title: Gradient-Free Bilevel Optimization for Architectural Hyperparameters of Nonsmooth Neural Networks
proposals.motivation: Hyperparameter optimization (HPO), especially for architectural choices in deep networks, remains a critical and computationally expensive challenge. Standard gradient-based bilevel methods struggle when the inner-level training involves nonsmooth activations (e.g., ReLU) or nonconvex losses, leading to biased or unstable hypergradient estimates. A robust, gradient-free approach is needed to reliably optimize architectures for modern, nonsmooth networks without restrictive smoothness assumptions.
proposals.hypothesis: A zeroth-order, gradient-free bilevel optimization algorithm, leveraging bandit feedback techniques, can efficiently and provably converge to approximate stationary points for the architectural hyperparameters of nonsmooth neural networks, circumventing the need for problematic hypergradient computations.
proposals.method: We will formulate architectural HPO as a bilevel problem where the outer objective is validation loss and the inner problem is nonsmooth network training. We will develop a novel algorithm combining inexact hypergradient descent principles with deterministic zeroth-order optimization techniques, using function value queries (bandit feedback) to estimate descent directions for the outer variables. Complexity analysis will be provided, drawing on tools from nonsmooth nonconvex optimization and online learning in markets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first provably convergent, gradient-free bilevel optimization method tailored for nonsmooth neural architecture search. It will extend the theoretical understanding of HPO beyond smooth settings and offer a practical algorithm for optimizing architectures where gradient-based meta-learning fails.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the integration of gradient-free, bandit-style optimization into the bilevel framework for nonsmooth networks, a significant departure from hypergradient-based methods which require implicit differentiation and inner-problem smoothness.

proposals.title: Robust Bilevel and Equilibrium Learning under Model Misspecification: From Linear Approximations to General Dynamics
proposals.motivation: A foundational assumption in bilevel optimization, equilibrium learning, and dynamic market design is known, linear transition dynamics. This is rarely true in practice, where models are approximations (e.g., in economic markets or complex control systems). Pre-2026 algorithms for these settings are fragile to such misspecification, leading to unstable training, suboptimal equilibria, or poor regret bounds when deployed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by explicitly modeling the discrepancy between assumed linear dynamics and true nonlinear dynamics as a structured, nonsmooth uncertainty set, we can develop robust first-order algorithms that converge to stable, near-optimal solutions even under significant model misspecification.
proposals.method: We will formulate bilevel and equilibrium-finding problems with a misspecified linear dynamics component as a robust optimization over a Wasserstein ambiguity set centered at the linear approximation. Building on recent work in inexact hypergradient descent and deterministic nonsmooth nonconvex optimization, we will develop a primal-dual algorithm that interleaves inner-loop equilibrium computation (via bandit feedback techniques for market settings) with outer-loop robustification steps. The core technical challenge is controlling the complexity and bias introduced by the uncertainty while maintaining convergence guarantees.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first provably robust algorithms for bilevel optimization and equilibrium learning under dynamical model misspecification. It will extend the theoretical toolkit for analysis in misspecified settings and offer practical methods for economic and robotic systems where true dynamics are complex and only linearly approximated.
proposals.novelty: While robust optimization exists, its integration into the bilevel and equilibrium learning frameworks—particularly for bandit feedback settings—to handle foundational linearity assumptions is novel. The approach moves beyond simple regularization by formally linking the uncertainty in dynamics to the convergence properties of hypergradient and equilibrium computation loops.

proposals.title: Efficient Bilevel Optimization for Scalable Action Selection in Continuous and Combinatorial Spaces
proposals.motivation: Decision-making in large or continuous action spaces, such as in dynamic pricing, resource allocation, and complex matching markets, remains a fundamental scalability bottleneck in machine learning. Pre-2026 methods often rely on restrictive linear approximations or discretization, which fail to capture complex value functions and lead to exponential growth in computational complexity. This limits the application of learning algorithms to real-world economic and robotic systems where action spaces are inherently high-dimensional.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that framing large-action decision problems as a bilevel optimization task—where an upper-level policy is learned through hypergradients of a lower-level smooth value function approximator—can yield scalable, sample-efficient algorithms with strong theoretical convergence guarantees, even for nonsmooth and nonconvex settings.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel inexact hypergradient descent algorithm where the lower-level problem approximates the Q-value function using a neural network trained with a spectral normalization penalty for Lipschitz continuity. The upper-level policy update will leverage deterministic nonsmooth optimization techniques, such as a carefully designed zero-order gradient estimator, to handle the inherent nonconvexity. Complexity analysis will focus on the trade-off between approximation error (epsilon) and computational cost, drawing from recent advances in bandit feedback for market equilibria to structure the action sampling process.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified algorithmic framework for decision-making in expansive action spaces with provable convergence rates. It will contribute new theoretical insights into the complexity of bilevel learning in nonsmooth settings and deliver a practical tool for applications like high-frequency trading and large-scale recommendation systems where action spaces are continuous or combinatorially large.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of bilevel optimization—typically used for hyperparameter tuning—with deterministic nonsmooth solvers and bandit-style feedback for direct action-space policy learning, a non-obvious synthesis beyond the prevalent use of bilevel methods for meta-learning or data reweighting alone.

proposals.title: Provably Efficient Bilevel and Equilibrium Learning with Overparameterized Neural Networks
proposals.motivation: Provably efficient algorithms for bilevel optimization and equilibrium learning (e.g., in markets) are typically restricted to linear or simple function classes, limiting their applicability to modern, high-dimensional problems. Extending these guarantees to nonlinear function approximators like neural networks is a critical open challenge, as it would bridge the gap between theoretical algorithmic design and practical deep learning systems in domains such as meta-learning and strategic interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: By leveraging the implicit regularization and optimization geometry of overparameterized neural networks, a Frank-Wolfe-type or inexact hypergradient algorithm can be adapted to achieve sublinear regret or approximation error in bilevel and equilibrium learning settings, even with nonsmooth losses.
proposals.method: We will adapt the inexact hypergradient framework from recent bilevel optimization work to handle neural network parameterizations. The core technical approach involves analyzing the trajectory of stochastic gradient descent on the inner problem to bound the bias and variance of the hypergradient estimate. We will employ tools from neural tangent kernel (NTK) theory and deterministic nonsmooth nonconvex optimization to control the complexity, ensuring the overall algorithm maintains provable efficiency in a bandit feedback market or nested optimization setting.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first provable efficiency guarantees for a class of bilevel and equilibrium learning problems using general neural network approximators. It will yield a practical algorithmic framework with theoretical foundations, advancing the design of learning systems for complex, data-driven strategic environments.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious element is the synthesis of NTK-based optimization theory with the complexity-aware, market-driven algorithmic frameworks pioneered by Jordan's group, moving beyond linear models while retaining rigorous convergence and regret bounds in structured, interactive settings.

proposals.title: Decoupling Horizon Dependence: A Variance-Reduced Frank-Wolfe Approach for Episodic Reinforcement Learning
proposals.motivation: In episodic reinforcement learning (RL), state-of-the-art regret bounds for linear MDPs and related settings exhibit polynomial dependence on the episode horizon H, often as H^(3/2) or H^2. This dependency is a fundamental bottleneck for applying RL to long-horizon real-world problems, such as robotic control or sequential decision-making in complex environments, where H can be very large. Reducing this dependence is a central open problem in the theoretical foundations of RL.
proposals.hypothesis: By leveraging a novel combination of variance-reduced policy optimization and a Frank-Wolfe (conditional gradient) paradigm, we can decouple the regret's primary dependence on H from the dominant terms, achieving a regret bound where H appears only in lower-order terms or under a milder polynomial.
proposals.method: We will formulate the episodic RL problem as a bilevel or constrained optimization over the space of visitation distributions. The core algorithm will be a variance-reduced Frank-Wolfe method, inspired by recent advances in inexact hypergradient descent for bilevel optimization. We will employ a nested structure: an outer loop performing Frank-Wolfe updates on the visitation distribution (ensuring efficient projection via linear optimization oracles), and an inner loop using a variance-reduced estimator for the policy gradient or Q-function, designed to control the error propagation across the H-step horizon. The analysis will hinge on managing the complexity of this nested optimization in a nonconvex, nonsmooth setting.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first model-free RL algorithm with a regret bound that significantly improves the polynomial dependence on H, ideally to H^(1/2) or better in the leading term. It will also contribute a new algorithmic template marrying conditional gradient methods with variance-reduced policy evaluation, extending the toolkit for structured, large-scale sequential decision-making.
proposals.novelty: While Frank-Wolfe methods have been explored in RL for constrained policy spaces, their application specifically to attack horizon dependence via a visitation distribution perspective is novel. The key innovation is the integration of variance reduction techniques from bandit and optimization literature directly into the Frank-Wolfe update to control long-horizon error accumulation, a non-obvious synthesis given existing work.

proposals.title: Kernelized Optimistic Least-Squares Value Iteration for Nonlinear Markov Decision Processes
proposals.motivation: The Optimistic Least-Squares Value Iteration (LSVI) framework provides a principled approach to exploration in reinforcement learning with linear function approximation, but its reliance on linearity limits applicability to complex, nonlinear environments. Kernel methods offer a theoretically grounded and flexible framework for nonlinear approximation, yet their integration into provably efficient, exploration-aware RL algorithms remains a significant open challenge, particularly for large-scale problems.
proposals.hypothesis: By embedding kernelized least-squares or Gaussian process regression within the optimistic LSVI framework, we can develop a computationally tractable algorithm that achieves sublinear regret for a rich class of nonlinear Markov Decision Processes, leveraging the representational power of reproducing kernel Hilbert spaces while maintaining statistical efficiency.
proposals.method: We will develop Kernelized Optimistic LSVI (KO-LSVI), which uses kernel ridge regression to approximate the Q-function. The core technical challenge is maintaining optimism for exploration; we will derive a novel, data-dependent confidence set in the RKHS using self-normalized martingale inequalities. To address computational complexity, we will employ scalable approximations such as random Fourier features or the Nyström method, and analyze the resulting trade-off between statistical regret and computational cost, drawing on recent advances in bilevel optimization for efficient hyperparameter tuning.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first provably efficient kernel-based RL algorithm with explicit regret bounds for nonlinear MDPs, extending the theoretical guarantees of linear LSVI. It will also deliver a practical, scalable implementation, bridging the gap between the nonparametric flexibility of kernels and the demands of modern, large-scale sequential decision-making.
proposals.novelty: While kernel methods have been used in RL, their integration into the *optimistic* LSVI framework—which explicitly balances exploration and exploitation—is novel. The key innovation is constructing a tractable, optimistic bonus for nonlinear RKHS function classes, moving beyond linear or finite-dimensional representations.

proposals.title: Avoidance of Degenerate Critical Points in Nonconvex Optimization Without Global Lipschitz Gradient Assumptions
proposals.motivation: Convergence guarantees for gradient-based methods in nonconvex optimization heavily rely on the global Lipschitz continuity of the gradient, a condition often violated in modern machine learning models like deep neural nets and complex bilevel formulations. This theoretical-practical gap limits our understanding of algorithm behavior in realistic, high-dimensional settings. Establishing convergence to non-degenerate critical points under weaker, local, or component-wise conditions is a fundamental open problem.
proposals.hypothesis: By employing a careful coupling of gradient descent dynamics with local regularity conditions and perturbation analysis, one can prove that iterates avoid strict saddle points and converge to local minima even when the global Lipschitz gradient constant is unbounded or does not exist.
proposals.method: We will extend the framework of inexact hypergradient descent and deterministic nonsmooth nonconvex optimization, focusing on functions where the gradient map is locally Lipschitz or admits a definable subgradient structure (e.g., in o-minimal geometries). The core technical approach involves analyzing the stability of the stable manifold of saddle points under perturbations, using adaptive step-size rules informed by local gradient variation. We will develop a two-phase algorithm that first identifies regions of benign geometry and then applies a safeguarded gradient method, with complexity analysis rooted in the geometry of the loss landscape.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first general theoretical guarantees for gradient descent avoiding saddle points under local, rather than global, Lipschitz gradient conditions. It will bridge a key gap between classical optimization theory and the empirical success of deep learning, offering new tools for analyzing complex, non-smooth objectives in bilevel optimization and equilibrium problems in markets.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in moving beyond the restrictive and often unrealistic global Lipschitz assumption, which is a standard fixture in nonconvex optimization theory, by developing a new analytical framework based on local geometry and stability. This directly challenges a foundational assumption in the field.

proposals.title: Escaping Saddle Points in Constrained Nonsmooth Nonconvex Optimization: A Primal-Dual Framework with Market Applications
proposals.motivation: A core challenge in modern machine learning and economic systems is nonconvex constrained optimization, where first-order methods can become trapped in suboptimal saddle points. Pre-2026 work on saddle-point escape largely focuses on unconstrained smooth settings, neglecting the critical interplay between saddle geometry and hard feasibility constraints prevalent in problems like equilibrium computation in matching markets with complex regulations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a carefully designed primal-dual algorithm, incorporating a perturbed gradient oracle and a feasible-set projection mechanism, can provably avoid strict saddle points and converge to a local minimizer even for a class of constrained, nonsmooth, nonconvex problems.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-timescale primal-dual algorithm where the primal step performs a perturbed proximal update within the feasible set to escape saddles, inspired by techniques for unconstrained nonconvex optimization. The dual step manages constraints via Lagrange multipliers, ensuring iterates remain feasible. We will analyze its dynamics using the theory of inexact hypergradients and differential inclusions, establishing non-asymptotic convergence rates and a probability bound for saddle avoidance, extending the analysis in 'Deterministic Nonsmooth Nonconvex Optimization' to the constrained, stochastic setting.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first provable saddle-avoidance guarantees for a broad class of constrained, nonsmooth nonconvex problems. It will yield a practical algorithm with applications in learning equilibria in constrained markets and training neural networks with nonconvex constraints, bridging the gap between optimization theory and complex real-world decision systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in unifying saddle-point escape theory from unconstrained optimization with primal-dual methods for constrained problems, specifically tackling the nonsmooth case. This moves beyond prior work that either handles constraints but assumes convexity or avoids saddles but ignores hard feasibility requirements.

proposals.title: The Geometry of Failure: A Measure-Theoretic Characterization of Initializations Converging to Saddle Points in Nonconvex Optimization
proposals.motivation: A cornerstone result in nonconvex optimization is that gradient descent, from almost all initializations, avoids strict saddle points and converges to local minima. This 'almost all' guarantee, while powerful, leaves open the characterization of the pathological measure-zero set. Understanding this set is critical for high-stakes applications like deep learning and market design, where convergence to a saddle can represent catastrophic failure or an unstable equilibrium.
proposals.hypothesis: The set of initial points that converge to strict saddle points under gradient flow and its discrete approximations forms a stratified set of low-dimensional manifolds, whose geometry and measure can be explicitly characterized for broad classes of structured nonconvex problems.
proposals.method: We will employ tools from dynamical systems and geometric measure theory. First, we will analyze the stable manifolds of strict saddle points under gradient flow, establishing their dimensionality. Second, we will extend this to discrete-time algorithms (e.g., gradient descent, accelerated methods) using techniques from inexact hypergradient and nonsmooth analysis, quantifying how the discretization enlarges this set. Finally, we will apply this framework to specific problem classes from matching markets and bilevel optimization to derive concrete, verifiable conditions for saddle-point convergence.
proposals.contribution: This work will transform the qualitative 'almost all' guarantee into a precise geometric and measure-theoretic description of failure cases. It will provide practitioners with diagnostic tools to avoid pathological initializations and offer theorists a deeper structural understanding of optimization landscapes. The results will directly inform robust algorithm design for learning equilibria.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on proving convergence from almost everywhere, this proposal inverts the perspective to explicitly characterize the negligible but critical set of exceptions. It synthesizes Jordan group's expertise in optimization, markets, and complexity to move from probabilistic guarantees to deterministic geometric descriptions.

proposals.title: Avoidance of Saddle Points in Stochastic Gradient Descent: A Dynamical Systems and Differential Inclusion Analysis
proposals.motivation: The convergence of stochastic first-order methods to local minima, rather than non-degenerate saddle points, is critical for nonconvex optimization in modern machine learning. Prior deterministic analyses using dynamical systems theory do not directly apply to stochastic algorithms like SGD, which are fundamental to training large-scale models. A rigorous framework for proving avoidance in stochastic settings remains an open challenge with significant implications for optimization theory and practice.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the dynamical systems framework, extended via stochastic approximation theory and differential inclusions, can be used to formally prove that stochastic gradient descent (SGD) and its momentum variants avoid strict saddle points almost surely under standard assumptions.
proposals.method: We will model SGD as a stochastic approximation process whose mean flow is governed by the deterministic gradient ODE. Using the theory of stochastic differential inclusions and the stable manifold theorem, we will analyze the perturbed dynamics near saddle points. The core technical approach involves constructing a Lyapunov function for the associated limiting differential inclusion and leveraging results on non-convergence to unstable equilibria for stochastic processes, extending recent work on deterministic nonsmooth nonconvex optimization to the stochastic, smooth setting.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous avoidance guarantees for plain SGD using a dynamical systems lens, bridging a gap between deterministic theory and stochastic practice. It will yield new insights into the role of noise and step-size schedules in escaping saddle points, potentially informing the design of more efficient stochastic optimizers for nonconvex problems.
proposals.novelty: While avoidance proofs exist for noisy gradient descent with isotropic noise, applying the dynamical systems/differential inclusion framework to analyze the intrinsic, non-isotropic noise of SGD (arising from minibatch sampling) is novel. This connects Jordan's recent work on deterministic nonsmooth dynamics directly to the core stochastic algorithms of machine learning.

proposals.title: Avoiding Saddle Points in Nonsmooth Nonconvex Optimization via Generalized Stable Manifold Theory
proposals.motivation: First-order methods for nonconvex optimization can converge to saddle points, which are problematic in machine learning. While the stable manifold theorem provides a rigorous avoidance guarantee for smooth functions, many modern problems (e.g., with ReLUs, L1 penalties, or equilibrium constraints) are nonsmooth. Pre-2026 theory lacks a parallel framework for functions with non-differentiable saddles, limiting algorithmic guarantees.
proposals.hypothesis: The geometric intuition of the stable manifold can be extended to a broad class of nonsmooth functions using subgradient dynamical systems and variational analysis, leading to provable avoidance of non-differentiable saddle points under perturbed first-order methods.
proposals.method: We will model the dynamics of subgradient descent as a differential inclusion. Using tools from variational analysis and set-valued stability theory, we will define a generalized stable manifold for Clarke critical points. The analysis will focus on structured nonsmoothness (e.g., composite functions, max-pooling). Algorithmically, we will analyze and potentially design perturbed variants of proximal gradient methods and bundle methods, establishing convergence to local minima with high probability under mild conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first theoretical framework for saddle avoidance in nonsmooth, nonconvex optimization, bridging a significant gap between smooth optimization theory and practical nonsmooth problems. It will yield provable convergence guarantees for a class of perturbed first-order methods applied to problems in robust statistics and constrained learning.
proposals.novelty: Extending the geometric stable manifold concept to nonsmooth settings is novel; prior avoidance results rely heavily on smoothness or Hessian information. The approach synthesizes variational analysis with dynamical systems theory for optimization, a non-standard combination pre-2026.

proposals.title: Lyapunov-Based Analysis of Non-Convergence to Saddle Points in Nonsmooth and Bilevel Optimization
proposals.motivation: A major challenge in modern non-convex optimization, including bilevel problems and nonsmooth settings, is ensuring algorithms avoid saddle points and converge to meaningful local minima. Pre-2026 work often relies on strict saddle properties or probabilistic arguments, lacking deterministic, dynamical-systems-based guarantees for complex, structured problems common in learning and market equilibria.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by constructing problem-specific Lyapunov functions, we can rigorously prove that certain first-order and hypergradient-based dynamics are repelled from saddle points in deterministic nonsmooth and bilevel optimization settings, even when traditional smoothness assumptions fail.
proposals.method: We will leverage tools from control theory and nonsmooth analysis. The approach involves: 1) Formulating the dynamics of algorithms (e.g., inexact hypergradient descent, proximal methods) as differential inclusions or non-autonomous systems. 2) Constructing candidate Lyapunov functions that increase strictly along trajectories initialized near saddle points, using the problem's inherent structure (e.g., market utility, bilevel value function). 3) Integrating this with complexity analysis to quantify the rate of escape, linking the Lyapunov decrease to the algorithm's step-size and inexactness parameters (epsilon).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, deterministic framework for analyzing saddle-point avoidance, extending beyond gradient-dominated regions. It will yield stronger convergence guarantees for algorithms in matching markets with bandit feedback and nonsmooth bilevel optimization, directly impacting the design of more robust learning and equilibrium-finding methods.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in synthesizing Lyapunov stability theory—traditionally used in control—with the complexity-theoretic and market-driven optimization themes of Jordan's recent work, to address non-convergence in structured, non-smooth problems where Hessian-based analysis is inapplicable.

proposals.title: A Primal-Dual Ascent Framework for Saddle-Point Problems: Rates, Robustness, and Market Applications
proposals.motivation: Primal-dual algorithms for Lagrangian saddle-point problems are central to constrained optimization, distributed learning, and equilibrium computation in markets. However, their analysis is often fragmented, with separate treatments for smooth, nonsmooth, and stochastic settings. A unified framework, akin to the convergence theory for gradient descent, is lacking, hindering the systematic design of robust algorithms for complex, data-driven applications like online matching markets with bandit feedback.
proposals.hypothesis: A generalized dual ascent framework, incorporating inexact hypergradient and error-tolerance concepts from bilevel optimization, can provide unified non-asymptotic convergence rates for primal-dual methods on a broad class of nonsmooth/nonconvex saddle-point problems, particularly those arising in economic equilibria.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework where the dual update is viewed as an ascent step on a value function, with the primal variable acting as a hyperparameter. This leverages tools from bilevel optimization (e.g., inexact hypergradient descent) to model primal inaccuracies. We will instantiate this framework for specific algorithms, including variants for deterministic nonsmooth settings and for stochastic bandit feedback in matching markets. The analysis will focus on complexity bounds under varying levels of oracle precision and problem structure.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unifying convergence theory for primal-dual algorithms, linking concepts from bilevel optimization, nonsmooth analysis, and online learning. It will yield new, robust algorithms with explicit complexity guarantees for finding equilibria in markets and for constrained machine learning problems under realistic feedback assumptions.
proposals.novelty: The novel synthesis of the dual ascent perspective with inexact hypergradient analysis from bilevel optimization to create a general saddle-point convergence framework is non-obvious. Applying this lens to bandit learning in matching markets, where the saddle point encodes a competitive equilibrium, is a significant extension of prior work.

ideas.title: Analyzing APA Convergence via Analogies to Thermodynamic Relaxation
ideas.description: This research adapts solution methods from statistical mechanics to formally analyze the convergence of the Affine Projection Algorithm (APA). In thermodynamics, the relaxation rate of a system to equilibrium is analyzed using Lyapunov stability theory and linearization, yielding explicit relationships between microscopic constants and macroscopic convergence times. Analogously, we propose to model APA's iterative process as a discrete-time dynamical system. The core of the work is to construct an appropriate Lyapunov function (e.g., based on the Bregman divergence induced by the APA cost function) and perform a linear stability analysis around the Wiener solution. This approach will derive the spectral radius of the linearized update, providing a formal expression for the convergence rate as a function of the step-size, regularization parameter, and projection order. The sample complexity—the number of iterations or data samples required to achieve ε-accuracy—will then be directly quantified from this rate, offering new theoretical insights into APA's performance trade-offs.

ideas.title: Apply Adaptive Mesh Refinement from Computational Fluid Dynamics to Trust Region Policy Updates
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from Adaptive Mesh Refinement in Computational Fluid Dynamics. In CFD, AMR stabilizes simulations by focusing computational precision where the solution changes rapidly (e.g., near shocks). Similarly, in trust region-based RL policy updates, we can adapt the trust region's geometry dynamically. The adapted solution is to analyze the local curvature or sensitivity of the policy's performance landscape during optimization. The algorithm would then apply an anisotropic, adaptive trust region—tightening constraints along parameter dimensions prone to causing performance collapse and relaxing them along more stable dimensions. This provides finer control over policy updates than a single spherical or clipped constraint, enhancing stability and sample efficiency in Adaptive Policy Acceleration (APA) by preventing catastrophic updates while permitting aggressive learning in safe directions.

ideas.title: Theory-Grounded Empirical Analysis of APA Convergence: Bridging Formal Bounds to Practical Performance
ideas.description: This research conducts a dual theoretical and empirical investigation into the convergence properties of Affine Projection Algorithms (APA). It formally derives convergence rates and sample complexity under progressively relaxed assumptions, moving from ideal conditions to more realistic ones. Crucially, it designs a comprehensive benchmarking suite to empirically validate these theoretical predictions on synthetic data with known ground truth and on real-world signal processing and communications datasets. The final output is a set of practically-informed theoretical guidelines that specify when and why APA converges in practice, quantifying the gap between ideal theory and observed performance.

ideas.title: Implicit Relative Credit Assignment for Stable, Advantage-Free Policy Optimization
ideas.description: This method replaces explicit advantage functions with a learned model of implicit relative credit. An auxiliary network is trained concurrently with the policy to predict a low-variance, action-guidance signal, such as the outcome of a learned pairwise comparison between state-action pairs or a smoothed temporal difference residual. This signal provides the necessary counterfactual feedback for efficient policy updates while being simpler to train and integrate than a full advantage estimator. The approach maintains sample efficiency and stability comparable to advanced actor-critic methods but within a simplified, more robust optimization pipeline that avoids explicit value function baselines.

ideas.title: Online Learning for Strategic Reporting in Evolving Preference Environments
ideas.description: This research direction develops online learning algorithms for a principal who must aggregate information or decisions from self-interested agents in a dynamic environment. The agents can strategically misreport their private data or local models, and their preferences—which dictate their misreporting strategies—evolve over time. The core challenge is to design a mechanism that continuously updates its aggregation rule and incentive structure, learning from observed reports and limited feedback to robustly approximate the true social choice or optimal model despite persistent, adaptive manipulation attempts. This enables systems like federated learning or participatory sensing to maintain reliability when both the task and the participants' strategic behaviors are non-stationary.

ideas.title: Adaptive Preference Tuning via Adaptive Filter Theory
ideas.description: This idea applies the core solution from adaptive filter theory in signal processing—where algorithms like RLS continuously track non-stationary signals—to the problem of dynamic human preferences in online learning. The analogous solution involves recursive, real-time parameter updates that minimize error against a streaming signal. Adapted to this context, we treat human preference feedback as a time-series signal and use a modified RLS algorithm to recursively update the reward model or policy parameters. This approach enables fast, incremental adaptation to preference drift, incorporates a forgetting mechanism for outdated feedback, and provides formal convergence guarantees for the non-stationary learning process, offering a mathematically grounded method for continuous policy refinement.

ideas.title: Inverse Reinforcement Learning for APA: Recovering the Latent Reward from Policy Gradients
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to inverse problems in geophysics, specifically seismic tomography, to the challenge of recovering a reward function from a policy fine-tuned with APA (Advantage-Propagation Alignment). In tomography, a subsurface model is iteratively refined to match observed seismic data. Here, the 'observed data' is the final APA-trained policy. The 'forward model' is the known APA update rule, which maps a reward function to a policy via gradient-based optimization. The adapted solution is to perform inverse reinforcement learning by treating the APA process as a differentiable layer: initialize a proxy reward function, use it to simulate APA fine-tuning, compute the discrepancy between the resulting simulated policy and the given policy, and then backpropagate through the APA update steps to adjust the reward function parameters. This iterative, model-based inversion provides a principled method to infer the latent reward that guided the alignment, addressing the scaling duality of understanding what the alignment process actually optimized for.

ideas.title: Apply online learning for dynamic preferences

ideas.sourceTitle: Fine-Tuning Language Models with Advantage-Induced Policy Alignment

ideas.title: Latent Trust Region Policy Optimization for Discrete Action Stabilization
ideas.description: This approach stabilizes APA's policy updates by applying trust region methods not directly to the discrete action distribution, but to a continuous latent policy representation. A primary network learns to encode the discrete policy into a smooth, lower-dimensional latent space where standard continuous trust region optimization (e.g., constrained KL divergence) is tractable and effective. A separate, trainable projection head then maps from the stabilized latent policy to the final discrete action logits, ensuring practical updates. This hybrid architecture guarantees monotonic improvement in the latent strategy while maintaining the expressiveness needed for complex discrete decision-making.

ideas.title: Uncertainty-Aware Robust Meta-Learning with Safe Adaptation Gates for Preference Alignment
ideas.description: This approach enhances standard meta-learning for fast adaptation by embedding a formal model of uncertainty to guard against misleading updates from small, noisy, or adversarial samples. The system maintains a Bayesian belief over the parameters of the new human preference distribution, enabling it to quantify the confidence of any proposed adaptation. Adaptation proceeds only when the evidence passes strict statistical gates for certainty and consistency, otherwise triggering a fallback to a conservative, high-coverage policy or initiating an active query for more informative human feedback. This results in a system that retains meta-learning's sample efficiency while being robust to the pitfalls of few-shot learning in non-stationary or adversarial environments.

ideas.title: Uncertainty-Conservative Adaptive Preference Aggregation for Offline RLHF
ideas.description: This approach adapts Adaptive Preference Aggregation (APA) for offline RLHF by explicitly quantifying and accounting for uncertainty in the human feedback. The system learns an ensemble of reward models and aggregation weight predictors from the fixed dataset, enabling it to estimate epistemic uncertainty for any given state-action pair. Optimization then proceeds using a pessimistic, uncertainty-penalized objective that prevents the policy from exploiting overfitted or ambiguous preference signals. This results in a robust policy that leverages APA's strengths for differentiating feedback quality while remaining safe and generalizable within the constraints of offline data.

ideas.title: Trust-Region Policy Optimization with Endogenous, Agent-Specific Accuracy Budgets
ideas.description: This research direction integrates trust region methods from continuous RL with mechanisms for private agent valuations of model accuracy. The core innovation is to treat an agent's trust region budget—the allowable divergence for policy updates—as a resource that is endogenously determined and traded based on the agent's private accuracy valuation. This creates a synergistic system where high-valuation agents can 'purchase' larger trust regions for more aggressive, potentially higher-reward updates, while low-valuation agents operate with tighter, more conservative constraints, stabilizing the collective learning process. The resulting market dynamics for trust region capacity couple individual learning stability directly to heterogeneous agent preferences, a link neither original idea proposed.

ideas.title: Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning for Preference Distribution Shifts via Gradient-Based Adaptation
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computational immunology, where model-agnostic meta-learning (MAML) is used to pre-train models for rapid adaptation to new virus variants. The analogous solution involves training a base model on a broad distribution of tasks (virus variants) so it can quickly specialize with minimal data. Adapted to APA, we propose using MAML to meta-train the agent on a wide spectrum of simulated human preference distributions. The core model parameters are learned to be highly sensitive to new preference data, enabling fast adaptation—with only a few gradient steps and minimal new human feedback samples—when the agent encounters a novel human preference distribution, thus solving the sample-efficiency challenge in dynamic alignment.

ideas.title: Synthesizing Preference Trajectories via Imitation of Expert Curation
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core methodology of paleoclimate reconstruction—synthesizing a complete, coherent historical sequence from sparse, fixed proxy data—to the problem of scaling APA (Adaptive Preference Aggregation) in offline RLHF. In paleoclimatology, scientists use data assimilation and inverse models to find the most likely climate trajectory consistent with all offline proxy records. Analogously, we propose treating a fixed dataset of human comparisons as indirect, noisy evidence of a latent reward function. The solution is to use a learned dynamics model and a policy parameterization as the forward model, and develop an offline assimilation algorithm (e.g., a smoothed variational method) that iteratively refines a synthetic trajectory of preferences, rewards, and policy updates. This process enforces global consistency across the entire dataset, mitigating the compounding error and overfitting issues typical in offline RL, thereby allowing APA's preference aggregation mechanisms to scale effectively using only the static dataset.

ideas.title: Hyper-Scale Multimodal Policy Alignment via Distributed Advantage Learning
ideas.description: This research fuses Advantage-Induced Policy Alignment (APA) with massively distributed training to tackle the grand challenge of aligning hyper-scale, unified multimodal models (e.g., models generating text, images, audio, and video from a single architecture). The core synergy is a novel distributed training framework that parallelizes the critical APA components—specifically, the collection of multimodal preference data, the computation of per-modality and cross-modal advantages, and the subsequent policy updates—across a vast, heterogeneous compute cluster. This approach makes it computationally feasible to align a single colossal model to nuanced human preferences across all modalities simultaneously, rather than aligning separate, smaller models. The outcome is a benchmark for the alignment efficiency and final performance of a single, coherent, and human-aligned giant model versus a federation of specialized smaller models.

ideas.title: Meta-Trained Offline Adaptation for Human Preference Alignment
ideas.description: This research direction develops a meta-learning framework where an agent is pre-trained on a diverse collection of offline human preference datasets, each representing a different preference distribution or task. The meta-objective is to learn an initialization and an adaptation algorithm that, given a new, small, and static offline dataset from a novel preference distribution, can quickly fine-tune the policy to align with those new preferences without any online interaction. This fusion creates a system capable of sample-efficient, offline adaptation to new human values, making RLHF more practical and safe for deployment where online exploration is costly or prohibited. The core challenge is meta-optimizing for robust policy updates under the constraints of offline learning and potential distributional mismatch.

ideas.title: Trust-Region-Guided Robust Preference Alignment with Biased Reward Models
ideas.description: This research direction investigates the integration of trust region methods, specifically from continuous control RL like TRPO or PPO, into APA to perform robust policy optimization under explicitly flawed reward models. The core synergy is that the trust region is repurposed to bound policy updates based on a learned estimate of the reward model's local reliability or bias, preventing over-optimization of deceptive rewards. This enables the system to strategically explore within trust regions where the reward signal is more trustworthy, or to seek additional feedback specifically for states where the reward model's bias is estimated to be high. The outcome is an alignment approach that is both stable and inherently robust to systematic imperfections in the preference or reward model.

ideas.title: Multi-Objective Preference Alignment from Random Initialization
ideas.description: This research investigates whether the benefits of alignment procedures like APA (e.g., stability, reduced reward hacking) persist when aligning a model from a random initialization towards multiple, potentially conflicting, human preference objectives. By removing the strong inductive bias of pre-training, the study can isolate how the alignment process itself negotiates and represents competing preference criteria in a model's nascent parameter space. This allows for a foundational analysis of the Pareto front's geometry and the emergence of compromise behaviors in a controlled setting. The results could inform more robust alignment strategies for future models trained from scratch and reveal inherent trade-offs in multi-objective value learning.

ideas.title: Inferring Reward Functions via Inverse RL with a Biophysical Prior
ideas.description: This idea combines Adversarial Policy Adaptation (APA) with inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) to infer reward functions from human demonstrations. Drawing an analogy from computational neuroscience and neuroeconomics, where internal utility functions are inferred from observed behavior under biophysical constraints, we adapt this solution. The adapted research idea uses a biophysically-informed prior within the IRL process. This prior, derived from models of human cognitive load, motor efficiency, or risk aversion, constrains the reward function inference. The result is a method that not only learns from demonstrations but also grounds the learned rewards in known human physiological and cognitive principles, leading to more robust and interpretable policy adaptation.

ideas.title: Hyper-Scale Online Preference Alignment for Living Foundation Models
ideas.description: This research fuses massively distributed training with online learning algorithms to create a new paradigm for aligning hyper-scale models. The core idea is to architect a distributed training system across thousands of GPUs that can continuously ingest streaming human feedback and perform efficient, incremental policy updates, treating the entire user base as a dynamic, real-time training signal. This enables a single 'living' foundation model to personalize its behavior and outputs for diverse, evolving user preferences at a global scale. The key challenge is designing a system that maintains training stability and model coherence while undergoing perpetual, distributed adaptation.

ideas.title: Federated Adaptive Parameter Alignment for Massive-Scale Foundation Models
ideas.description: This research proposes and empirically validates Federated Adaptive Parameter Alignment (FAPA), a method for fine-tuning extremely large language models (e.g., 100B+ parameters) within real-world federated learning systems. It investigates the stability and communication efficiency of transmitting and merging only small, task-specific APA adapters across diverse, resource-constrained client devices and non-IID data distributions. The core novelty lies in scaling adapter-based personalization to a massive, cross-silo federated setting, tackling challenges like adapter divergence, secure aggregation for parameter-efficient modules, and system heterogeneity at an unprecedented model scale. The outcome is a validated framework for secure, efficient, and stable collaborative adaptation of frontier AI models.

ideas.title: Multimodal Preference Embedding Alignment (MPEA) via Contrastive Advantage Learning
ideas.description: This method extends policy alignment to multimodal generation by first learning a dense representation space for human preferences. A contrastive network is trained on pairwise human feedback to embed model outputs and exemplars, such that more preferred outputs are closer to their corresponding exemplars. The alignment phase then uses Advantage-Induced Policy Alignment, but with the advantage defined as the reduction in distance to a preferred exemplar in this learned embedding space, relative to a baseline output. This provides a rich, structured reward signal that prevents scalar reward hacking and aligns models with the nuanced, multi-aspect nature of human preferences for images, audio, and video.

ideas.title: Incorporate contextual information in contracts

ideas.title: Hyper-Scale Federated Training with Strategic Agent Populations
ideas.description: This research direction implements a hyper-scale federated learning framework, leveraging distributed training across thousands of GPUs, to train massive models with a realistic population of strategic agents. Instead of assuming uniform rationality, the system incorporates a complex mix of altruistic, adversarial, and rational agents, each with distinct objectives and capabilities. This allows for the first large-scale empirical analysis of how strategic heterogeneity affects convergence, model integrity, and the efficacy of aggregation and defense mechanisms at a planetary scale. The outcome is a new understanding of the stability and governance requirements for future decentralized AI systems built on inherently non-aligned participant pools.

ideas.title: Stability-First Adaptive Activations with Efficient Distributed Validation
ideas.description: This research first rigorously benchmarks the Adaptive Polynomial Activation (APA) function against a suite of tasks designed to stress-test optimization stability, generalization, and long-context reasoning at standard model scales (e.g., 1B-10B parameters). Upon identifying a stable, superior configuration, the project designs a lightweight, communication-efficient wrapper for APA that integrates with existing hyper-scale training pipelines, adding minimal synchronization overhead. The final phase validates the approach by training a model at extreme scale (hundreds of billions of parameters), using the efficiency gains from the improved activation to justify the cost, rather than making scale the initial goal.

ideas.title: Robust Preference Alignment with Guarded Online Learning and Dynamic Stability Gates
ideas.description: This research designs a system that safely integrates online learning for dynamic preferences by preventing harmful policy drift. It employs a dual-policy architecture where a protected foundational model anchors stable values and a malleable policy undergoes online updates. A meta-learned gating network, which assesses the volatility and certainty of incoming preference signals, controls the blending of knowledge from both policies to the active agent. This approach ensures continuous adaptation to genuine preference evolution while robustly safeguarding against catastrophic forgetting of fundamental alignment objectives.

ideas.title: Analyze higher-order critical points

ideas.sourceTitle: First-order Methods Almost Always Avoid Saddle Points

ideas.title: Multi-Objective Preference Alignment via Pareto Optimization
ideas.description: This research adapts a core solution from Evolutionary Multi-Objective Optimization (EMOO) to the challenge of aligning AI with multiple, potentially conflicting, human preferences. The analogous problem—finding optimal solutions when improving one objective worsens another—is solved in EMOO by algorithms like NSGA-II, which identify a Pareto frontier of non-dominated solutions. We adapt this by framing APA not as a search for a single aligned policy, but as an optimization process that discovers and maintains a diverse frontier of model policies. Each policy on this frontier represents a distinct, optimal trade-off between the conflicting preference criteria (e.g., creativity vs. factual accuracy, compliance vs. autonomy). The final system would include a mechanism to navigate or select from this frontier, enabling context-aware alignment without collapsing multi-faceted human values into a single compromised point.

ideas.title: Decentralized Inverse Reinforcement Learning from Aligned Policies
ideas.description: This research direction investigates how a group of decentralized agents, each possessing a policy fine-tuned via methods like APA (Advantage-Weighted Policy Alignment), can collaboratively recover the underlying reward function that guided their individual alignment. By developing peer-to-peer inference mechanisms, agents can exchange observations of policy behavior or gradient information to solve the inverse reinforcement learning problem collectively. This allows the system to establish and refine a consensus reward model without any agent or central coordinator having privileged knowledge of the true objective. The result is a trust-minimized alignment framework where cooperative emergence of a shared goal replaces top-down reward specification.

ideas.title: Federated Scaffolding for Robust On-Device Preference Alignment
ideas.description: This system enables robust, miniaturized preference alignment for small language models on edge devices by overcoming the data poverty problem. It combines an on-device APA fine-tuner with a secure federated learning layer that draws from a dynamically curated central reservoir of synthetic and anonymized preference data. This reservoir provides essential diversity and anti-overfitting signals, preventing the model from collapsing due to narrow local feedback. The result is a practical alignment system that maintains personalization while ensuring stability and mitigating the risks of bias amplification from sparse edge data.

ideas.title: Multi-Agent APA via Consensus Dynamics in Opinion Networks
ideas.description: This idea scales Adversarial Policy Alignment (APA) to multi-agent settings by drawing an analogy from sociophysical models of opinion dynamics. The analogous solution from opinion dynamics, like the DeGroot model, shows how simple local averaging rules can lead to global consensus in a network of agents. This is adapted by treating each language model's policy parameters as an opinion vector. In a collaborative or competitive setting, agents periodically update their policies by taking a weighted average of the policies of other agents they have recently interacted with, where the weighting is influenced by the adversarial feedback from the APA process (e.g., favoring policies from interactions that received higher alignment scores). This decentralized consensus mechanism, operating under the overarching adversarial scaffold of APA, allows the multi-agent system to converge on aligned policies without requiring a single, monolithic training process, effectively scaling alignment to complex interaction networks.

ideas.title: Distributed APA for Federated On-Device Alignment
ideas.description: This research fuses hyper-scale distributed training with edge-device miniaturization to develop a federated APA framework. A central coordinator orchestrates the distributed training of a massive, shared APA meta-model across thousands of GPUs, using techniques like decentralized optimization. This meta-model is then distilled into ultra-lightweight APA modules deployed on edge devices for local, private fine-tuning of small language models. The key innovation is a bidirectional feedback loop where anonymized alignment gradients or policy updates from the decentralized edge fine-tuning are aggregated to continuously and privately improve the central APA meta-model, creating a self-improving, scalable alignment ecosystem.

ideas.title: Tractable and Auditable Multi-Concept Framework for Large-Scale Collaborative Value Distribution
ideas.description: This research designs a practical system for profit sharing in large-scale collaborative AI projects by directly addressing the computational infeasibility of classic cooperative game theory solutions. It employs statistically rigorous approximation methods for the Shapley value, coupled with techniques to disentangle and attribute value to specific contributions within a complex model. The system's output is then validated through an audit layer that compares the proposed distribution against other core solution concepts, ensuring the final profit-sharing scheme is both computationally tractable and robust to theoretical critique.

ideas.title: Alignment from Random Weights: Testing APA's Efficacy Without Pre-trained Priors
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that Adversarial Preference Alignment (APA) requires a capable, pre-trained model as a starting point. Drawing an analogy from developmental biology, where organoids are grown from undifferentiated stem cells using precise environmental cues, we investigate if APA's benefits hold when fine-tuning starts from a randomly initialized model. The adapted idea is to design and test a structured 'alignment differentiation protocol'—a specific curriculum of reinforcement learning signals, data ordering, and auxiliary objectives—that can guide a model from a random state directly toward aligned and capable behavior, bypassing the need for pre-trained priors. This explores the fundamental conditions necessary for alignment to emerge de novo.

ideas.title: Hyper-scale APA with Distributed Training via Astrophysical N-body Simulation Methods
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to the extreme-scale parallelization problem from computational astrophysics—specifically, cosmological N-body simulations of galaxy formation—to the challenge of implementing and benchmarking APA (Adaptive Parameter Allocation) with massively distributed training. The analogous field's solution uses hierarchical domain decomposition (tree codes) and hybrid MPI/thread parallelization to manage billions of interacting particles across thousands of compute nodes, efficiently handling both local computations and long-range force interactions. The adapted idea applies this by structuring the distributed training process for extreme model sizes as a dynamic, hierarchical spatial system: model parameters and optimizer states are treated as 'particles' distributed across GPU clusters. A tree-based communication protocol, inspired by Fast Multipole Methods, will synchronize gradient updates, managing near-field (intra-node) and far-field (inter-node, inter-cluster) dependencies with optimized, asynchronous communication. This approach aims to solve the key bottlenecks of hyper-scale distributed training—communication overhead, load imbalance, and fault tolerance—enabling the benchmarking of APA frameworks for trillion-parameter models across heterogeneous, geographically distributed infrastructure, much as astrophysicists simulate the universe by breaking it into manageable, interacting domains.

ideas.title: Robust Alignment via Ensemble and Calibration of Imperfect Reward Models
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from weather forecasting to address the challenge of aligning AI with imperfect or biased reward models. In climate science, individual models have known biases and flaws, so forecasters employ multi-model ensembles and calibration techniques to produce a consensus that is more accurate and robust than any single source. Similarly, this project proposes to study APA's robustness by not using a single flawed reward model, but by designing and evaluating a system that employs an ensemble of diverse, biased reward models. These models would be calibrated and their outputs aggregated—perhaps through learned weighting based on performance on a small, high-quality validation set—to create a more reliable composite reward signal, thereby making the alignment process more resilient to the imperfections of any individual component.

ideas.title: Decentralized Direct Preference Alignment (D2PA)
ideas.description: This research investigates a decentralized framework for training AI agents using direct human feedback, specifically combining the Adversarial Preference Alignment (APA) method with decentralized incentive mechanisms. The core challenge is to enable a network of agents to collectively optimize their policies based on sparse, direct human preference ratings, without a trusted central coordinator to aggregate feedback or maintain a canonical reward model. The synergy lies in designing peer-to-peer protocols where agents propagate and validate human feedback, using cryptographic or game-theoretic mechanisms to incentivize honest reporting and prevent sybil attacks, thereby achieving robust preference alignment in a fully decentralized setting. This could enable the development of aligned AI systems in open, permissionless networks where no single entity controls the training data or objective.

ideas.title: Adaptive Pruning for On-Device Alignment via Neuromorphic Sparsity
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from computational neuroscience and neuromorphic engineering—specifically, the brain's energy-efficient, sparse, and event-driven communication—to the problem of miniaturizing Anthropic's Alignment via Process-based Assistance (APA) for on-device use. The analogous solution uses dynamic sparsity, where only a salient subset of neurons activates per task. Adapted to this context, the idea is to create a lightweight version of APA that does not fine-tune the entire small language model. Instead, a minimal on-device module would analyze the immediate interaction (e.g., a harmful prompt or a user correction) to identify and apply alignment updates to only a sparse, critical subset of the model's parameters. This event-driven, highly targeted approach dramatically reduces computational and memory overhead, enabling real-time, personalized alignment on resource-constrained edge devices.

ideas.title: Immunological Trust Adaptation for Federated Learning Agents
ideas.description: This research inverts the assumption of rational agents in federated learning by drawing an analogy from immunology. The immune system solves the analogous problem of distinguishing helpful from harmful entities (self cells, commensal bacteria vs. pathogens) not with a fixed rule but through contextual evaluation by dendritic cells, which integrate multiple positive and negative signals. We adapt this solution by proposing an 'immunological trust layer' for the federated learning server. This layer mimics dendritic cell function, continuously evaluating each participating agent based on a multi-signal profile—including update consistency, impact on global model performance, local data characteristics, and optionally declared cooperative or adversarial intent—to dynamically classify agent behavior. The server then adapts its aggregation strategy, potentially rewarding altruistic agents, isolating or correcting adversarial ones, and standardly aggregating rational ones, thereby creating a more robust and behaviorally-aware federated learning system.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Mutualism Metrics for Federated Learning Agent Reputation
ideas.description: This idea adapts solutions from theoretical ecology, specifically the analysis of mutualistic networks, to the problem of scoring and incentivizing reliable federated learning agents. In ecology, networks like those between plants and pollinators use metrics such as 'dependence asymmetry' and 'interaction strength' to quantify the reliability and net benefit of interactions between species, which promotes network stability. The adapted solution applies this concept by designing a decentralized reputation system where an agent's score is a function of analogous metrics—calculated from the historical asymmetry and proven utility (e.g., improvement in global model accuracy) of its contributions versus its consumption of the shared model. This biologically-inspired approach incentivizes agents to act as reliable, productive 'mutualists' within the federation, thereby enhancing overall network robustness and learning efficiency.

ideas.title: Peer-Predicted Inverse Reinforcement Learning for Scalable and Truthful Reward Inference
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a novel framework for inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) in multi-demonstrator, crowdsourced settings. The core innovation is to integrate peer prediction mechanisms directly into the IRL process, treating each human demonstration as a 'report' to be verified. Demonstrators are incentivized to provide high-quality, truthful behavioral data through peer prediction contracts that reward consistency with peers' underlying reward models, not just superficial action matching. This allows the system to filter out noisy, malicious, or low-skill demonstrations and to robustly aggregate a unified reward function, enabling scalable and reliable reward learning from non-expert populations where ground truth is unavailable.

ideas.title: Empirical validation via a federated testbed network modeled on ecological observatories
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from ecology, where large-scale observatory networks are used for empirical validation of models in complex, real-world systems, to the context of federated learning. The analogous problem is validating theoretical models in distributed, heterogeneous, and non-controlled environments. The adapted research idea is to implement a 'Federated Learning Testbed Network' (FLTN), mirroring an ecological observatory. By establishing a consortium of real-world partner organizations (e.g., hospitals, mobile networks) that deploy standardized, minimally intrusive software agents, researchers can run coordinated, longitudinal experiments across truly heterogeneous data silos. This testbed network would employ common protocols for logging system metrics, data distributions (via secure statistics), network conditions, and model performance, thereby generating a rich, empirical dataset to rigorously validate proposed federated learning mechanisms—such as novel aggregation algorithms, personalization techniques, or incentive structures—under practical constraints and scale.

ideas.title: Validating APA on VLMs via Controlled, Multi-Stage Stress Testing
ideas.description: This idea adapts the validation approach from structural engineering to the problem of testing Adapter-based Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (APA) on very large-scale models (VLMs). In structural engineering, novel materials are validated for large-scale construction through controlled, incremental stress testing (e.g., in wind tunnels) to predict failure modes. The adapted research idea proposes a multi-stage, controlled stress-testing protocol for APA. This involves designing a series of fine-tuning experiments on VLMs where variables like learning rate schedules, projection dimensions, and task complexity are systematically and independently varied. The goal is to meticulously monitor for stability failure analogs—such as loss spikes, activation drift, or catastrophic forgetting—across different model scales and architectures. This method aims to move beyond simple performance benchmarking to build a predictive understanding of APA's stability boundaries and performance benefits under the extreme 'load' of fine-tuning massive parameter sets, thereby de-risking its application to future state-of-the-art models.

ideas.title: Disentangling Capability Acquisition from Alignment Steering
ideas.description: This research investigates if the benefits of fine-tuning alignment techniques like APA depend on the model's pre-existing capabilities. We start from a randomly initialized model and subject it to a two-phase process: first, a controlled, alignment-conscious pre-training phase to build basic task competency, then an alignment fine-tuning phase. This design separates the effects of learning capabilities from steering them, testing if alignment techniques are effective when applied during foundational learning. The outcome clarifies whether these methods primarily correct misaligned objectives or can actively shape them from inception.

ideas.title: Direct Preference Optimization via Human-in-the-Loop Calibration
ideas.description: This research investigates APA's performance when using direct human ratings instead of a separately trained reward model, drawing an analogy from psychophysics. In sensory measurement, direct magnitude estimation bypasses intermediate models by having subjects assign numerical values to stimuli, with calibration protocols (e.g., cross-modality matching) and statistical normalization (z-score transformation) used to align raw judgments into a consistent scale. We adapt this solution by implementing a real-time calibration layer for APA that normalizes raw human preference scores—accounting for individual rater bias, inconsistency, and scale variation—and feeds the stabilized signal directly into the policy update. The core investigation is whether such a carefully calibrated direct human feedback loop can match or exceed the performance of the conventional two-stage RLHF pipeline that relies on a separately trained proxy reward model, potentially simplifying alignment pipelines and reducing approximation errors.

ideas.title: Adversarially-Instrumented Federated Testbed for Resilience Validation
ideas.description: This research designs and implements a federated learning testbed that embeds programmable adversarial agents into real-world deployments to proactively evaluate mechanism robustness. The system enables controlled injection of strategic client behaviors—such as data poisoning, update manipulation, and collusive attacks—into an otherwise operational federated network. This approach allows for the empirical measurement of defensive mechanisms not just under typical conditions, but under coordinated stress tests that mirror sophisticated real-world failures. The result is a validation framework that quantifies resilience and exposes failure modes before they are exploited in production systems.

ideas.title: Cross-Modal Advantage Distillation for Multimodal Alignment
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea of applying Advantage-Induced Policy Alignment (APA) to multimodal tasks by drawing an analogy from biomedical signal fusion. In that field, aligning disparate signals like EEG and ECG is achieved by projecting them into a common latent space and using reliable modalities to guide others. The adapted solution proposes 'Cross-Modal Advantage Distillation.' A shared encoder aligns text, image, audio, and video into a unified embedding space. The well-defined APA framework is first applied strictly in the text domain to generate robust advantage signals. These advantages train a reward model within the shared latent representation. Finally, this latent reward model is used to perform policy alignment on the non-text generative models (for images, audio, video) via distillation, effectively transferring the alignment signal from the structured text modality to the more complex, high-dimensional modalities.

ideas.title: Apply Ecosystem Service Valuation Methods for Collaborative Model Profit Sharing
ideas.description: This idea adapts solution concepts from ecological economics, specifically ecosystem service valuation, to the problem of profit sharing in collaborative machine learning models. In environmental science, the total economic value of an ecosystem service (like water purification by a wetland) must be distributed among the various natural and human contributors. Analogously, the total profit generated by a collaborative AI model must be distributed among contributing data providers, algorithm developers, or compute resource contributors. By adapting methods like value attribution frameworks, we can develop a systematic approach to calculate the marginal economic contribution of each participant's input to the model's final, profitable output. This goes beyond simple performance metrics (like Shapley value for accuracy) to directly link contribution to the realized financial value, ensuring a fair distribution that reflects actual impact on the collaborative venture's profitability.
